book_metadata.title: Betrayal
book_metadata.subtitle: Power, deceit, and the fight for the future of the Royal family
book_metadata.author_line: From the number one bestselling author Tom Bower

cover.image_alt: A white background, with an image of Meghan Markle and Prince Harry cut diagonally. A roundel features with an image of the Royal Family and text that reads ‘author of the explosive Royal biography Revenge’. A quote from The Sunday Times in the bottom right hand corner reads ‘A master of intricacies’. The top line reads ‘From the number one bestselling author Tom Bower’, followed by the title, Betrayal in gold. Beneath, in black, the subtitle reads ‘Power, deceit, and the fight for the future of the Royal family’.

paragraphs.text: The fate of Harry and Meghan, the Duke and Duchess of Sussex, still commands global attention. People are less unified by sympathy or dislike than by curiosity: will they flourish or merely survive? Debate centers on an imagined endgame — divorce, debt, or disaster — and a steady stream of commentary that treats Harry as Meghan’s dependent rather than the other way around. Even sympathetic coverage notes how public Meghan’s life has become; she has been prolific on social media and appeared to relish Harry’s devotion in a Harper’s Bazaar interview. Speculation about the marriage has followed them since their 2020 move to California, but so far the dire predictions have not come true.

The couple’s story is driven by an almost constant search for publicity. Meghan stages frequent, polished appearances while building a commercial brand rooted in her version of personal truth. Five years ago my book Revenge challenged that narrative, showing how she left people behind and reshaped events to suit her rise. That arc reached a peak with her 2021 interview with Oprah Winfrey. Since then she has often acted independently of Harry, appearing together only when it served publicity or profit, and pursuing ambitious commercial projects from California.

Harry has struggled in parallel. His bestselling memoir Spare and outspoken interviews have created controversy he refuses to acknowledge, and his public charity work has been strained by distance from Britain and by critics who see him trading on royal status to earn fees. The couple’s choices have occurred against a backdrop of a Royal Family weakened by scandal and illness, leaving both the Sussexes and the monarchy at a crossroads.

Because of the dramatic events and new disclosures about their lives before Megxit, this book returns to their story with a fresh look. In many ways 2026 will be a make-or-break year for them all. The narrative begins in the momentous week of Queen Elizabeth II’s death in September 2022.

Manchester, 5th September 2022. In the countdown to Meghan Markle’s speech, Ella Robertson read the Autocue and groaned, “She’s fucked it.” Meghan had promised to speak about One Young World, but her prepared remarks focused almost entirely on herself: in ten minutes she planned to say “I” nearly fifty times. The moment felt like a betrayal of the conference’s purpose and of the organizers’ careful planning.

One Young World, founded in 2010, brings thousands of young leaders together to discuss global challenges. Kate Robertson and her daughter Ella had been nervous about inviting Meghan because ‘Megxit’ was still fresh and the couple’s return from California had stirred intense publicity. Older Britons were angry, but many younger attendees admired the Sussexes as symbols of resistance to a racist establishment; the Robertsons believed Meghan would attract and energize the audience.

The organizers asked for guarantees that the appearance would not become a showcase for the Sussexes. Ella sought reassurance from James Holt, the couple’s chief of staff, who promised nothing controversial was coming. Holt did not volunteer that Meghan was launching podcasts and about to publish an interview—moves aimed more at expanding her celebrity than at the charity’s agenda. Meghan agreed, in principle, to focus on women’s empowerment and to avoid self-promotion and expensive clothing, but past behavior made the Robertsons uneasy.

Meghan’s history with One Young World showed why. In 2014 Misan Harriman had recommended the then-unknown actress, predicting she would be a star. In Ottawa in 2016, already dating Harry, she pushed for VVIP treatment, demanded inclusion in official photographs, and left upset without delivering a scheduled talk; her publicist Gina Nelthorpe-Cowne was left in tears. Negotiations for the 2019 Royal Albert Hall event were chaotic: palace staff sent mixed signals, and One Young World agreed to fund a £200,000 scholarship so Meghan could mention it on stage. Five months after Archie’s birth she appeared brittle but professional and introduced Harry as a surprise guest; the audience welcomed them as champions of women and girls.

Meanwhile, Meghan’s post-royal career depended on expert media management in Los Angeles. Her in-house team—Beth Herlihy, Miranda Barbot and Ashley Hansen—worked with outside PR Keleigh Thomas Morgan of Sunshine Sachs. Hansen, who billed herself as a chief architect of the Sussexes’ PR, monitored press coverage constantly and planned tight control over the Manchester appearance. Meghan asked Vogue editor Edward Enninful for a cover timed to the trip, a request that strained their talks because she sought heavy editorial control.

Hansen and the Sussexes demanded strict event conditions: tight ticketing to exclude protesters, no journalists in the hall, and permission only for trusted allies—Omid Scobie and photographer Misan Harriman—to attend. They even arranged for Sky to cut to Meghan live while Westminster announced a new prime minister. Days before the event Harry complained about security, saying the Home Office had refused armed protection in Manchester and London. After professional styling by Charlotte Tilbury at their hotel, the couple walked into Bridgewater Hall to 2,300 young people. A van played “God Save the Queen” and there were a few boos from outside.

On stage Meghan wore red and smiled as Harry clapped beside her. She abandoned the billed theme of ethical leadership, instead praising One Young World for seeing in her “what I wanted to see fully in myself” and then speaking almost entirely about her own rise. Ella Robertson recognized the familiar pattern: Meghan could not follow the brief. The couple left hastily—worried about missing a train—leaving organizers and many Britons with the impression that the Sussexes felt the country and its critics irrelevant.

The appearances continued without pause. The next day the Invictus executives flew the couple to Düsseldorf on a military flight; they were greeted like heads of state and Harry was happy to be feted. Back in London at the WellChild awards he reverted to the popular, personable prince, praised for his warmth with sick children. He then returned to Frogmore Cottage, anticipating the long-planned return to Los Angeles and the next stage of the Sussexes’ managed public life.
Global Celebrity

Meghan Markle met Prince Harry on a blind date she arranged in July 2016 while she was working at Wimbledon. She was 34, facing the end of her role on Suits and a breakup with her then-boyfriend; she had even told her publicist she planned to freeze her eggs. Meeting Harry transformed her prospects almost overnight.

They quickly moved from Soho House in London to a safari in Botswana. Over several days in the bush Meghan listened as Harry listed his problems and vulnerabilities; she responded with sympathy in a way his previous partner, Cressida Bonas, had not. Whereas Bonas grew exhausted by Harry’s conviction that he was watched and threatened, Meghan appeared to validate his feelings. Within weeks the relationship hardened into mutual commitment, and within months palace aides were whispering that she had ‘found a meal ticket’—a charge that deeply offended Harry.

Harry long resented the royal hierarchy, saying the institution had controlled and imprisoned him and denied him an identity beyond the ‘spare.’ Palace officials, sensing his frustration, tried to prepare Meghan for royal life before their wedding on May 19, 2018. They arranged instruction about the Church of England, a thirty-point dossier from Harry’s private secretary explaining patronages and constitutional neutrality, and coaching by senior courtiers. Harry would later write, “Meghan wasn’t given one minute of training.”

Despite those efforts, senior officials quickly saw that Meghan expected to set her own terms. An adviser met her monthly and warned that she resisted the deference and compromise the role required. Meghan admired the Duchess of Windsor’s independence and made clear she would not be a submissive royal. She treated the family’s traditions and mystique as obstacles rather than responsibilities and assumed she could change the rules if necessary.

Meghan arrived with difficult family relations of her own and showed little interest in repairing them. Weeks before the wedding she told aides, ‘I hope my father won’t be coming,’ and did not involve him in arrangements; he was not invited to the ceremony and later became a media problem after staged photographs and lies. Palace officials concluded both sides had unrealistic expectations: Meghan would not accept limits, and the family assumed she would somehow fit in without changing her behavior.

Her celebrity instincts clashed with royal protocol. Meghan spoke of the monarchy as if it were a studio and treated its public role like a platform for celebrity causes. She took initiative on wedding choices without consulting the Queen, who disliked some decisions—reportedly calling the dress ‘too white’ for a divorcee—and Meghan later omitted the monarch from a Vogue guest list of ‘Game Changers.’

A telling moment came at the College of Arms, where she initially asked for a coat of arms motif described as a ‘songbird with its tongue cut out.’ Heralds were stunned; she ultimately agreed to a nightingale without the silencing detail. Her wedding, a lavish public spectacle that cost millions, confirmed her status as a global celebrity: she rode through Windsor with practiced Hollywood poise and drew large, diverse crowds.

But the celebration exposed tensions. Her guest list favored Hollywood acquaintances over some of Harry’s oldest friends, and she appeared uncomfortable with the rowdier aspects of his social circle. Within months she complained of being underappreciated for limited official duties; palace enthusiasm had cooled as friction with William and Kate and other senior figures increased. The chapter closes with the sense that initial romance and celebrity had collided with a stubbornly different culture and that both sides underestimated how hard integration into the royal family would prove.
